description: HLLQP - LIFE 07 QZ - Taxation of Life Insurance and Tax Strategies - Section 3
examTips: Focus on policy transfers, spousal/child rollovers, income attribution, leveraging strategies, charitable donations, and annuitization.

questions.question: How is an absolute assignment of a life insurance policy to a spouse treated for tax purposes?
options: A) It triggers an immediate 50% tax penalty.
B) The spouse must pay tax on the entire cash value.
C) It qualifies for an automatic tax-free "rollover," transferring at the policy's ACB.
D) The policy must be surrendered and restarted.
optionFeedback: Spousal transfers don't incur penalties.
Spouses don't pay immediate tax.
It qualifies for an automatic tax-free "rollover," transferring at the policy's ACB.
The policy continues without interruption.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the transfer (to spouse).
Step 2: Apply the rule: Spouse transfers qualify for automatic tax-free rollover at ACB value.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. No penalties; spouse assumes ACB, not current CSV; no surrender required.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: It qualifies for an automatic tax-free "rollover," transferring at the policy's ACB.

questions.question: If a policy is transferred to a spouse under the automatic rollover, what happens under the "income attribution rules" if the recipient spouse later surrenders the policy for a gain?
options: A) The recipient spouse pays the tax.
B) The tax is forgiven completely.
C) The original transferring spouse must report the policy gain and pay the tax.
D) The insurance company pays the tax.
optionFeedback: Income attribution rules may override normal assignment rules.
Tax is not forgiven in attribution situations.
The original transferring spouse must report the policy gain and pay the tax.
The insurance company does not pay policy gains.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the attribution rule application.
Step 2: Apply the rule: Original transferor reports gain if attribution rules apply, despite spouse's surrender.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Recipient spouse doesn't report gain; tax isn't forgiven; insurer doesn't pay.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: The original transferring spouse must report the policy gain and pay the tax.

questions.question: Under what specific conditions can a life insurance policy roll over tax-free to a child?
options: A) Only if the child is over 21 and the policy has no cash value.
B) If the policyholder receives no money for it AND the life insured by the policy is the child.
C) Any policy can roll over to a child at any time.
D) Only if the child pays fair market value for it.
optionFeedback: Age and cash value are not the determining factors.
If the policyholder receives no money for it AND the life insured by the policy is the child.
Conditions do apply for tax-free transfers.
Paying fair market value would create a taxable gain.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify conditions for tax-free rollover to child.
Step 2: Apply the rule: Tax-free only if (1) no consideration paid AND (2) child is the life insured.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Age and cash value are irrelevant; not all transfers qualify; fair market value creates tax.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: If the policyholder receives no money for it AND the life insured by the policy is the child.

questions.question: If a policyholder dies and they are NOT the life insured on the policy, how is the policy treated?
options: A) The policy pays out the death benefit.
B) The policy is deemed disposed of just before death, potentially triggering a policy gain on their final tax return.
C) The policy is canceled without value.
D) The government takes ownership of the policy.
optionFeedback: The policy doesn't simply pass on; it's a disposition event.
The policy is deemed disposed of just before death, potentially triggering a policy gain on their final tax return.
The policy has value and is deemed disposed of.
The government doesn't seize policies.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the situation (owner dies, not the insured).
Step 2: Apply the rule: Deemed disposition occurs just before death; gain calculated and reported on final return.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Policy isn't simply paid out; it's disposed of; it has value; government doesn't seize.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: The policy is deemed disposed of just before death, potentially triggering a policy gain on their final tax return.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of naming a "contingent policyholder" (successor owner)?
options: A) It doubles the death benefit.
B) It allows the policy to bypass the deceased owner's estate, avoiding probate fees and delays.
C) It eliminates all income tax forever.
D) It allows the policy to be sold for twice its value.
optionFeedback: Contingent ownership doesn't double benefits.
It allows the policy to bypass the deceased owner's estate, avoiding probate fees and delays.
Contingent ownership doesn't eliminate all taxes.
It doesn't affect policy sale value.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the purpose of contingent policyholder.
Step 2: Apply the benefit: Direct transfer to successor avoids probate delays and fees.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. No doubled benefits; taxes not eliminated; sale value unaffected.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: It allows the policy to bypass the deceased owner's estate, avoiding probate fees and delays.

questions.question: If a business owner uses a partial assignment of their life insurance policy as collateral for a business loan, what tax benefit might they receive?
options: A) They can deduct the entire loan amount from their taxes.
B) They can deduct a portion of the premiums paid (limited to the Net Cost of Pure Insurance) as a business expense.
C) The death benefit becomes a tax deduction.
D) They do not have to pay the loan back.
optionFeedback: The loan itself is not fully deductible.
They can deduct a portion of the premiums paid (limited to the Net Cost of Pure Insurance) as a business expense.
Death benefits are not deductible.
Loans must be repaid.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the situation (business use of policy collateral).
Step 2: Apply the rule: NCPI portion of premiums may be deductible as business expense.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Loan not fully deductible; death benefit not deductible; loans must be repaid.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: They can deduct a portion of the premiums paid (limited to the Net Cost of Pure Insurance) as a business expense.

questions.question: What does it mean to "annuitize" the Cash Surrender Value (CSV) of a life insurance policy?
options: A) Cashing out the policy to buy a series of ongoing income payments (an annuity).
B) Changing the policy from whole life to term life.
C) Adding an annual bonus to the death benefit.
D) Transferring the policy to a spouse annually.
optionFeedback: Cashing out the policy to buy a series of ongoing income payments (an annuity).
Annuitization doesn't change policy type.
Annuitization doesn't add bonuses.
Annuitization is not an annual transfer.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Define annuitization.
Step 2: Apply the concept: CSV is converted to an annuity product providing ongoing income.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Not a policy type change; no bonus addition; not a spousal transfer.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: Cashing out the policy to buy a series of ongoing income payments (an annuity).

questions.question: How does an "insured retirement strategy" (leveraging) generate tax-free cash for a retiree?
options: A) By surrendering the policy piece by piece.
B) By receiving policy dividends in cash.
C) By using the policy's CSV as collateral to secure a series of bank loans, which are received tax-free.
D) By lowering the face amount of the policy annually.
optionFeedback: Piece-by-piece surrender triggers tax on gains.
Dividend amounts are limited.
By using the policy's CSV as collateral to secure a series of bank loans, which are received tax-free.
Lowering face amount doesn't generate cash for retirement.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the strategy (leveraging/insured retirement).
Step 2: Apply the concept: CSV collateralizes bank loans; loan proceeds are tax-free; death benefit repays loan.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Piece-by-piece surrenders create tax; dividends are insufficient; face amount reduction doesn't help.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: By using the policy's CSV as collateral to secure a series of bank loans, which are received tax-free.

questions.question: What is the general limit on claiming the Charitable Donation Tax Credit in a normal tax year?
options: A) 100% of your net income.
B) 75% of your net income.
C) $50,000 maximum.
D) $200 maximum.
optionFeedback: The limit is not 100%.
75% of your net income.
There is no $50,000 cap.
The limit is not $200.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the donation credit limit.
Step 2: Apply the rule: In a normal year, the credit is limited to 75% of net income.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. 100% is too high; $50,000 and $200 are incorrect thresholds.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: 75% of your net income.

questions.question: If you assign a brand NEW life insurance policy to a registered charity, why can't the charity issue you an immediate tax receipt for the face amount?
options: A) Charities are not allowed to own life insurance.
B) Because a new policy has no Cash Surrender Value (CSV) yet, so it has no immediate value to the charity.
C) Because the death benefit is taxable.
D) Because new policies take 5 years to be approved by the CRA.
optionFeedback: Charities are allowed to own life insurance.
Because a new policy has no Cash Surrender Value (CSV) yet, so it has no immediate value to the charity.
The death benefit is not immediately taxable.
There is no 5-year approval requirement.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the issue (new policy to charity).
Step 2: Apply the rule: Tax receipt is based on the policy's current value (CSV), not future death benefit.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Charities can own policies; death benefit isn't immediately taxable; no 5-year CRA approval.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: Because a new policy has no Cash Surrender Value (CSV) yet, so it has no immediate value to the charity.
